title: A solvent-free method for the synthesis of block copolymers with fluorinated pendant groups by a hydrosilylation reaction
abstract:
A Solvent-Free Method for the Synthesis of Block
Copolymers with Fluorinated Pendant Groups by a
Hydrosilylation Reaction
J. D. JEYAPRAKASH S. SAMUEL,1 R. DHAMODHARAN,1 CHRISTOPHER K. OBER2
1 Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, Chennai 600 036, India
2 Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Cornell University, Ithaca, New York 14853
Received 28 October 1999; accepted 17 January 2000
ABSTRACT:
A solvent-free method for the hydrosilylation of pendant double bonds in
block copolymers is reported in this article. An anionically prepared block copolymer,
poly(styrene-b-1,2-butadiene), was heated with 1H,1H,2H,2H-perﬂuorooctyldimethyl-
hydrosilane in the presence of a nonacidic platinum catalyst for 24–26 h to obtain a
quantitatively hydrosilylated block copolymer. Gel permeation chromatography, IR,
and thermogravimetric analysis were used to characterize the block copolymers ob-
tained. © 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci A: Polym Chem 38: 1179–1183, 2000
Keywords:
block copolymer; hydrosilylation; solvent-free; ﬂuorinated block; styrene;
1,2-butadiene; Karstedt catalyst; perﬂuorohydrosilane; pendant double bond
INTRODUCTION
Block copolymers with pendant ﬂuorinated blocks
have recently been studied as important materi-
als for low-surface-energy coatings and liquid-
crystalline applications.1–4 Many polymeric ma-
terials used in low-surface-energy applications
involve either ﬂuorinated or siloxane functional-
ities. One of the methods used for the preparation
of these materials is the hydrosilylation reaction.
Thus, the hydrosilylation of pendant double
bonds (incorporated into a homo- or block copoly-
mer prepared through living anionic polymeriza-
tion methods) with either siloxane or perﬂuoro-
based silanes is one way of preparing polymeric
materials with special properties.5–7 The incorpo-
ration of perﬂuorinated or siloxane segments into
homo- and block copolymers via the hydrosilyla-
tion reaction with pendant double bonds has been
well reported. Guo et al.8,9 used chlorotris(triph-
enylphosphine) rhodium (I) in toluene at 110 °C
to hydrosilate polybutadiene (PB), styrene–buta-
diene copolymer, and nitrile–butadiene copoly-
mer; the reaction time was 20 h. There are other
reports concerning the hydrosilylation of pendant
double bonds with various hydrosilanes and hy-
drosilylation catalysts. Thus, the hydrosilylation
of poly(styrene-b-isoprene) with pentamethyldisi-
loxane via Karstedt’s catalyst (symmetrical divi-
nyltetramethyldisiloxane) in toluene at 110 de-
grees has been reported as well.10 The reaction
times were of the order of several days. The pen-
dant double bonds of hydroxyl-terminated PB
have been hydrosilylated with an amine-termi-
nated hydrosilane via Speier’s catalyst.11
a-Fluorinated oleﬁns have been hydrosilylated
with a polymeric siloxane containing pendant
SiOH bonds (hydrosilylation) with Co2CO8 as the
catalyst in toluene at room temperature.12 The
reaction time reported was uniformly 5 days. A
platinum catalyst has been used for this reaction
as well.13 The pendant double bonds in poly(sty-
rene-b-isoprene) have been hydrosilated with
Correspondence to: R. Dhamodharan (E-mail: damo@acer.
iitm.ernet.in)
Journal of Polymer Science: Part A: Polymer Chemistry, Vol. 38, 1179–1183 (2000)
© 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
1179
 tridecaﬂuorooctyldimethylchlorosilane in anhy-
drous toluene with reaction times around 10
days.14 The hydrosilylation of various ﬂuorinated
oleﬁns and a diene with different chlorosilanes in
the presence of a peroxide or a Pt catalyst in
hexane heated at 70 °C for 18 h has also been
reported.15
In this article, we report a novel observation,
the quantitative hydrosilylation of pendant dou-
ble bonds in a block copolymer without the use of
any solvent. The additional novelty of this report
is that the reaction times required were 24–26 h;
in several other reports, days were required for
complete conversion. Because of the importance
of the hydrosilylation of polymers with pendant
unsaturation, this report should enhance the
value of commercial polymers such as Kraton-D.
EXPERIMENTAL
Synthesis of Poly(styrene-b-1,2-butadiene)
The styrene block of the block copolymer was
synthesized by the anionic polymerization of sty-
rene in benzene with sec-butyllithium as the ini-
tiator, as reported previously.16 We prepared
blocks of the desired degrees of polymerization by
varying the monomer to the initiator molar con-
centrations. After the polymerization of the sty-
rene monomer, a small aliquot of the polymer
solution was transferred via a cannula to de-
gassed butanol for the purpose of estimating the
number-average molecular weight (Mn) of the
polystyrene (PS) block. This was followed by the
addition of a 10-fold molar excess of dry dipiper-
idinoethane (DIPIP; previously distilled over n-
butyllithium) as the initiator to ensure the 1,2-
type of butadiene addition as established previ-
ously.17 The required quantity of the liquid
butadiene monomer was then added from a cali-
brated Schlenk-type centrifuge tube (the mono-
mer was previously distilled trap to trap over
dibutylmagnesium and stored in the liquid form
at 225 °C). All transfers were done via a cannula.
The reaction was allowed to proceed for 2 h [we
earlier
established
with
gas
chromatography
(GC) that a reaction time of 2 h was more than
sufﬁcient to prepare a poly(1,2-butadiene) block
with an Mn of 42,000 in benzene with sec-butyl-
lithium as the initiator], after which the polymer-
ization was terminated by the addition of distilled
and degassed butanol.
1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, d, ppm): 1.34, 1.41
(OCH2), 1.74 (OCH, benzylic), 4.8 (1,2-oleﬁnic
terminal CH2), 5.6 (1,2- as well as cis- and trans-
1,4-oleﬁnic CH), 6.1, 6.5, 6.98 (aromatic CH). IR
(ﬁlm form, cm21) n: 3082, 3061, 3027, 3003 (aro-
matic and oleﬁnic COH stretching), 2922, 2849
(aliphatic COH stretching), 1640 (CAC, 1,2-),
1600 (CAC, aromatic), 1493, 1453 (COH bend-
ing, PS backbone), 995 (vinyl-1,2-deformation),
968 (trans-1,4-deformation as well as a contribu-
tion from PS), 911 (vinyl-1,2-deformation), 758,
700 cm21 (characteristic aromatic COH in plane
deformation peaks from PS).
Hydrosilylation of Poly(styrene-b-1,2-butadiene)
We next discuss a typical hydrosilylation reaction
using the block copolymer with 5 mol % butadiene
and 95 mol % styrene (Table I, Entry 2) as an
example. 500 milligrams of the polymer (0.277
mmol of PB or the 1,2-double bond) was mixed
with 0.1 mL of ﬂuorosilane (a slight excess; the
actual requirement per stoichiometry is 0.06 mL)
in a single-necked ﬂask ﬁtted with a water-cooled
condenser. For the purpose of quantitating the
extent of the reaction with time, 0.2 mL of decane

In this article, we report a novel observation,
the quantitative hydrosilylation of pendant dou-
ble bonds in a block copolymer without the use of
any solvent. The additional novelty of this report
is that the reaction times required were 24–26 h;
in several other reports, days were required for
complete conversion. Because of the importance
of the hydrosilylation of polymers with pendant
unsaturation, this report should enhance the
value of commercial polymers such as Kraton-D.

tiator, as reported previously.16 We prepared

centrations. After the polymerization of the sty-
rene monomer, a small aliquot of the polymer
solution was transferred via a cannula to de-
gassed butanol for the purpose of estimating the
number-average molecular weight (Mn) of the
polystyrene (PS) block. This was followed by the
addition of a 10-fold molar excess of dry dipiper-
idinoethane (DIPIP; previously distilled over n-
butyllithium) as the initiator to ensure the 1,2-
type of butadiene addition as established previ-
ously.17 The required quantity of the liquid
butadiene monomer was then added from a cali-
brated Schlenk-type centrifuge tube (the mono-
mer was previously distilled trap to trap over
dibutylmagnesium and stored in the liquid form
at 225 °C). All transfers were done via a cannula.

Hydrosilylation of Poly(styrene-b-1,2-butadiene)
We next discuss a typical hydrosilylation reaction
using the block copolymer with 5 mol % butadiene
and 95 mol % styrene (Table I, Entry 2) as an
example. 500 milligrams of the polymer (0.277
mmol of PB or the 1,2-double bond) was mixed
with 0.1 mL of ﬂuorosilane (a slight excess; the
actual requirement per stoichiometry is 0.06 mL)
in a single-necked ﬂask ﬁtted with a water-cooled
condenser. For the purpose of quantitating the
extent of the reaction with time, 0.2 mL of decane
GPC Characteristics of the Polymers Before and After Hydrosilylation
The mole percentage of the styrene block: 100 minus the mole percentage in Column 2.
was added (the reaction works equally well with-
out the decane). 10 microliters of the Karstedt
catalyst (Huls America; donated by Thomas J.
McCarthy of University of Massachusetts, Am-
herst, Massachusetts) was injected into the reac-
tion ﬂask, which was placed in an oil bath main-
tained between 100 and 120 °C. A small quantity
of the sample was withdrawn, diluted with 0.1
mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF), and analyzed by
GC. At t 5 0, the ﬂuorosilane/decane ratio was
0.39 6 0.01. Between 24 and 26 h, it was 0.15
6 0.01, suggesting that 0.06 mL of the ﬂuorosi-
lane, or essentially the required molar quantity of
the ﬂuorosilane, had reacted with the polymer.
A Bio-Rad Win-IR Fourier transform infrared
(FTIR) spectrophotometer was used for recording
IR spectra. A Waters gel permeation chromatog-
raphy (GPC; styragel columns HR3, HR4, HR5)
along with a millennium V2.5 data-analysis pack-
age was used for the determination of the Mn and
polydispersity index (PDI). Thermal analysis was
performed with a Du Pont 2000 V5.1A thermal
analyzer between ambient temperature and 800
K at a heating rate of 25 °C/min under a ﬂowing
nitrogen atmosphere. A Jeol GSX400 (Japan; 400
MHz for proton) NMR spectrometer was used to
record 1H NMR spectra. GC analysis was per-
formed with a Nucon gas chromatograph (Chen-
nai, India) interfaced with a computer. The data
analysis was performed with an Aimil data sta-
tion (Chennai, India). The column used was 10%
OV 17; the oven temperature was 50–160 °C at a
heating rate of 10 °C/min, the injector tempera-
ture was 140 °C, and the detector temperature
Poly(styrene-b-butadiene) with a butadiene block
of about 90% 1,2-microstructure was obtained by
the anionic polymerization of styrene followed by
the addition of butadiene and DIPIP.17 Although
the quantities of styrene and butadiene used were
based on the size of the blocks desired, DIPIP was
used in a 10-fold molar excess as that of the ini-
FTIR spectra of PS-b-1,2-PB5 before hy-
drosilylation (below) and after hydrosilylation (above).
FTIR spectra of PS-b-1,2-PB5 before hy-
drosilylation (below) and after hydrosilylation (above).
HYDROSILYLATION OF PENDANT DOUBLE BONDS
tiator sec-butyllithium. In all the proton NMR
spectra, the peak due to the 1,2-vinylic methylene
protons (4.8 ppm) was the major peak in the ole-
ﬁnic region. A small peak due to the methine
protons of the 1,2- as well as the 1,4-microstruc-
ture was also seen around 5.6 ppm. The percent-
age of the 1,2- and 1,4-microstructures was eval-
uated from the integrated areas. Because the de-
gree of polymerization of the butadiene block
prepared was small, we wished to ascertain for all
the block copolymers prepared the precise ratio of
the 1,2-microstructure to the 1,4-microstructure
in the block copolymer under our experimental
conditions. Toward this end, a control experiment
was performed in which a homopolymer of buta-
diene was prepared under conditions identical to
those used for the block copolymer synthesis. The
PB thus prepared exhibited peaks at 1.15, 1.19
(OCH2, aliphatic), 1.92 (OCH, allyllic), 4.8 (mul-
tiplet, oleﬁnic OCH2, 1,2-), and 5.2 (multiplet,
oleﬁnic OCH, 1,2- and 1,4-). The integration of
the areas under the peaks for the 1,2- and 1,4-
microstructures suggests that the 1,4-microstruc-
ture constituted less than 10 mol % of the PB,
greater than 90 mol % of the PB.18 This experi-
added essentially in the 1,2-microstructural form
in the block copolymer under our experimental
conditions. This is consistent with results re-
ported previously.17 The GPC characteristics of
the synthesized polymers are shown in Table I.
The molar percentage of the butadiene block was
evaluated from the difference in the Mn’s of the
block copolymer and the PS prepared in the same
experiment. These values are approximate esti-
mates because coil dimensions of PS and block
copolymer do not need to be the same in a given
solvent, so a calibration curve based on the coil
dimensions of PS would not likely precisely reﬂect
a change in the molecular weight.
The IR of the block copolymer exhibited the
characteristic peaks due to PS and PB. IR spec-
tral data indicated the predominance of the vinyl-
1,2-microstructure in the block copolymer by vir-
tue of the higher relative intensities of the peaks
due to vinyl-1,2-deformation at 995 and 911 cm21
compared to that due to the trans-1,4-deformation
that appeared at 968 cm21. Because the PS block
contributed to the deformation peak at around
968 cm21 as well, this conﬁrms the NMR ﬁnding
that the microstructure of the butadiene block of
the block copolymer was almost exclusively 1,2-.
The absence of the peak at 729 cm21 in the IR
shows conclusively that the cis-1,2-microstruc-
ture was not present in the block copolymer.19
The block copolymers were heated with a slight
excess of 1H,1H,2H,2H-perﬂuorooctyldimethyl-
hydrosilane20 in the presence of the Karstedt cat-
alyst (a nonacidic Pt catalyst) between 100 and
120 °C. Initially, the reactions were done in the
presence of an equal volume of decane as the
ﬂuorosilane. This was done to quantitate the time
at which the hydrosilylation reaction went to
completion via GC. Very near quantitative con-
versions were achieved between 24 and 26 h, as
revealed by the disappearance of the ﬂuorosilane
peak in GC. The IR of the hydrosilylated block
copolymer before and after the reaction for 24–26
h is shown in Figures 1–3. The disappearance of
the peaks at 1640 cm21, due to the CAC stretch-
ing vibration of the 1,2-vinylic double bond [seen
clearly in Fig. 2], and at 911 cm21, due to in-plane
vinylic-1,2-deformation [seen clearly in Fig. 3],
This ﬁnding is consistent with the GC data. The
reaction went to completion between 24 and 26 h
in the absence of decane (the internal standard
used for GC) as well, a fact conﬁrmed by IR spec-
FTIR spectra of PS-b-1,2-PB5 before hy-
drosilylation (below) and after hydrosilylation (above).
The Mn went down by a value of ;400 after
hydrosilylation (see Table I), whereas the PDI was
unchanged. An increase in the molecular weight
was expected after the hydrosilylation reaction. We
attribute the decrease in the molecular weight to
the lyophobic nature of the ﬂuorinated block. The
ﬂuorinated block probably formed a dense core to
minimize its interaction with THF, resulting in a
decrease of the coil dimensions compared to the
unmodiﬁed block copolymer and, therefore, a de-
crease in the molecular weight. A thermogravimet-
ric analysis (TGA) of one of the ﬂuorinated blocks
(PS-b-1,2-PB2) is shown in Figure 4. The initial
decomposition temperature changed from 350 °C to
a value close to 450 °C after the introduction of
pendant ﬂuorinated moieties, suggesting an im-
In view of the quantitative nature of the hy-
drosilylation reaction in the absence of a solvent,
the methodology reported in this article should
enhance the value of commercial polymers with
pendant double bonds, such as Kraton-D, in low-

18. If x is the degree of the polymerization of the 1,2-
microstructure and y is the degree of the polymer-
ization of the 1,4-microstructure, it follows that the
area under the peak at 4.8 ppm is equal to 2x and
that the area under the peak at around 5.6 ppm is
x 1 2y. For the control experiment, we observed
integrated areas of 19.2 for the peak at 4.8 ppm and
10.6 for the area at 5.6 ppm. This works out to a
value of 9.6 for x and 0.5 for y.
